Load balancer SKU consistency: 100% of tenant load balancers use the Standard (Regional) SKU, which shows an operational preference for the Standard feature set and health-probe model, so plan the internal Azure Load Balancer as Standard SKU with health probes and backend pools to match tenant networking behavior and diagnostics expectations.

VM identity standardization: nearly all virtual machines use combined SystemAssigned and UserAssigned managed identities (≈98.8%), which indicates managed identities are the accepted mechanism for VM-to-Azure-service access, so assign a managed identity (system and/or user-assigned) to the jumpbox and use it for Key Vault and logging access rather than embedding credentials on the VM.

OS platform skew toward Windows: ~95% of VMs report Windows as the OS, which means Windows management workflows are dominant in the tenant, so if your team expects to use Windows tooling provision a Windows jumpbox image by default and only choose Linux if you explicitly need SSH-based tooling and then ensure SSH public keys are managed centrally.

Network security groups are pervasive and regionally concentrated: there are ~957 NSGs with a strong concentration in eastus (~65%), indicating NSGs are the standard mechanism for tier-level filtering, so create and apply dedicated NSGs per tier (subnet or NIC) consistent with existing rules and place the jumpbox in the same regional design to simplify rule reuse and auditing.

Hub-and-spoke networking pattern is common: virtual network peering names like 'spoke2-hub' and 'AppGWtoAKSVnetPeering' show spokes peered to shared hubs, which implies traffic funnels through a central management or transit VNet, so position the jumpbox in the hub or a management spoke and rely on VNet peering for internal connectivity to ILB-backed application tiers.

Private name resolution and private endpoints are widely used: the tenant has many Private DNS zones and private endpoints, showing a preference for internal name resolution for service discovery, so register the internal Load Balancer DNS records in the tenant Private DNS zones and link them to the VNets used by the tiers for reliable name resolution.

Bastion usage exists but is not universal: only seven Bastion hosts exist and the Developer SKU is the most common, which indicates Azure Bastion is used selectively, so prefer Azure Bastion where available to avoid public jumpbox exposure and otherwise build the jumpbox without a public IP and restrict management access via NSG and jumpbox-to-bastion alternatives.

Key Vault protection conventions: key vaults consistently have soft-delete and enablePurgeProtection enabled (soft-delete typically 90 days, purge protection true), which shows tenant emphasis on secure secret lifecycle, so store jumpbox secrets (SSH keys, privileged credentials) in Key Vault and grant the jumpbox managed identity least-privilege access rather than placing secrets on disk.

Monitoring and diagnostics integration: many workloads are connected to Operational Insights / Log Analytics workspaces (149 workspaces), indicating centralized telemetry, so enable VM diagnostics and forward jumpbox logs and metrics to the tenant Log Analytics workspace to align with existing monitoring and incident workflows.
